skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Use when creating, updating, or improving agent skills.
skill.summary: A dispatcher skill for skill creation excellence. Provides decision trees, templates, and standards for writing high-quality AI agent skills.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a documentation and reference skill providing guidance on writing AI agent skills. All static findings are false positives - the scanner detected patterns in markdown documentation (code formatting with backticks, mentions of cryptographic terms, URLs in documentation) rather than actual executable code. The render-graphs.js is a build script for documentation generation, not runtime functionality.

content.value_statement: This skill provides templates, decision trees, and best practices for creating and improving AI agent skills. It helps users avoid common mistakes and follow standardized patterns.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through skill creation using decision trees
Provides templates for different skill types (technique, reference, discipline, pattern)
Includes CSO (Search Optimization) guidance for skill discoverability
Offers anti-rationalization techniques for rules that agents follow
Provides testing guidelines for skill validation
limitations: Does not execute code - purely informational reference
Requires manual skill creation following provided templates
Does not validate skill functionality automatically
Does not include actual skill implementations

use_cases.title: Creating a New Skill
use_cases.description: Use the decision tree to select the right architecture tier and template for a new skill, then follow the template structure.

use_cases.title: Improving Existing Skills
use_cases.description: Diagnose why a skill is not being triggered or ignored by agents, then apply anti-rationalization or CSO techniques.

use_cases.title: Standardizing Team Skills
use_cases.description: Ensure all team skills follow consistent naming conventions, metadata standards, and directory structures.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to create a new skill called [skill-name]. It should [what it does]. Use the Tier 1 template from the writing-skills reference.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Skill Triggering
prompt_templates.prompt: My skill [skill-name] is not being triggered by Claude. The description is: [description]. Apply CSO optimization from writing-skills.

prompt_templates.title: Add Anti-Rationalization
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a discipline skill with rules that Claude keeps ignoring. The rules are: [rules]. Apply anti-rationalization techniques.

prompt_templates.title: Structure Complex Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to create a complex skill with multiple concepts (200-1000 lines). Use Tier 2 expanded architecture with proper references.

output_examples.input: I want to create a skill for handling API errors
output_examples.output: A complete SKILL.md with: name: api-error-handler, description: 'Use when API request fails or returns error status', metadata with triggers: api-error, network-failure, 503, with references/ folder for detailed handling procedures

output_examples.input: My skill description is 'This skill helps with file operations' - it's not triggering
output_examples.output: Optimized description: 'Use when user asks to read, write, or modify files. Triggers on: file-create, file-edit, file-delete, touch, mkdir, rm command'

best_practices: Always start descriptions with 'Use when...' followed by specific trigger conditions
Include at least 3 keywords in metadata.triggers for better discoverability
Keep single-file skills under 200 lines; use references/ for longer content
Split monolithic skills into multiple files using the tier system
anti_patterns: Describing workflow instead of triggers (e.g., 'This skill helps you create files' instead of 'Use when user wants to create a file')
Using generic skill names like 'helper' or 'utility' instead of descriptive gerunds
Missing metadata.triggers field or having fewer than 3 trigger keywords
Creating long monolithic SKILL.md files instead of modular references/

faq.question: What is CSO in skill writing?
faq.answer: CSO stands for 'Concept Search Optimization'. It is the practice of writing skill descriptions that maximize the likelihood of being triggered by matching user intent patterns.

faq.question: What is anti-rationalization?
faq.answer: Anti-rationalization refers to techniques that make skill rules difficult for agents to ignore or rationalize away. It involves making rules specific, unavoidable, and tied to concrete outcomes.

faq.question: What is the difference between Tier 1 and Tier 2 skills?
faq.answer: Tier 1 skills are simple, single-file skills under 200 lines. Tier 2 skills are complex, multi-concept skills between 200-1000 lines that use a references/ directory structure.

faq.question: How do I test my skill?
faq.answer: Use the RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycle: Write a test case (RED), implement the skill to pass (GREEN), then refactor for clarity and efficiency.

faq.question: What triggers should I include?
faq.answer: Include specific keywords, error messages, command names, and symptoms that users would type. Aim for at least 3-5 diverse triggers per skill.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for any AI tool?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill provides guidance that applies to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other AI agent systems that support skill-based functionality.
